RT @NeerajKA: Mummies are a form of prosciutto
@gakonst Holochain is an agent-centric network in which each agent has his own data associated to a "smart contract", instead of all data being exactly the same for everyone.

@gakonst When agents interact, they leave signed entries in each others "smart contract" that works as a trusted trace of the interaction.
E.g. Receiving 5 stars for staying in your house in an Airbnb-like smart contract.
@gakonst Surprisingly enough, a huge portion of the data of most apps and social networks does not need final consensus. E.g Twitter, Airbnb...

@gakonst The problem comes when you need one trusted global piece of data. On holochain is hard.

It's hard to prevent double spending, and it uses blacklists to prevent sybil attacks and misbehavior.

@gakonst The part that is similar to git is that you can exchange you source chain (sequence of signed and valid transactions) on a "smart contract" with other people, something like a push. And they can trust it to learn things about you.
@gakonst * does not need global consensus
@gakonst I am not a confirmed fan of holochain. It does seems to be exploring a new approach for using cryptographically signed integrations for human coordination

The story of the World Wide Web as a technology developed with public funds is just partially true. 👇
The Web was primarily a personal obsession of @timberners_lee, as an individual, and not a project conceived by any committee.
It was not conceived in a public research institute for computing or communication, but on CERN, a high energy physics laboratory.
@timberners_lee worked on it as a side project for a long time, and had a lot of trouble pushing it internally within CERN.

Public funding did provided @timberners_lee with a salary and the necessary cutting edge hardware. But, honestly, he was supposed to be using it for CERN direct problems, and not for creating a new technology for global comunication.
At the beginning, he even had to disguise the project as a tool for CERN, just to be able to work on it.
Very rapidly, the center of gravity moved toward the US, far away from European bureaucrats. Who were clearly unable to grasp the potential of the new technology.

Money is just #2. A unit of account. And not even.

Money is a measurement. A quantitative estimation of a real world phenomena. 

But what is the physical quantity it tries to estimate? 👇 https://t.co/VO13sMFt9X

Reputation. Reputation of having done "something of value" for someone else.

But value is subjective. How do you get one common unit of value?

That's when you need #1. 👇
#1 is a conscious cultural decision: we will make valuable things for one person only if that person has made valuable things for others.

When we transfer money to someone, we are paying back part of the debt "the community" (not us) had with that person.

Once all debt is paid, no one will do/give anything more for that person. (a.k.a. broke).

Note that this also means that the less value you recognize to others, the more things you will get done for you by others. This creates the subjective "scale" of the units of the measurement, decentralizedly, and out millions of subjective value exchanges.
#3 becomes pretty redundant and evident at this point. Of course that if someone did something of value yesterday, we, as a community, consider that debt still valid today.
Maybe this is just a way to rephrase the textbook definition, without adding anything new. But still. I find it easier to understand and reason around that the original 3 bullets.

My (today's) recipe for saving the world: 
- @proofofhumanity 
- @RadxChange-like Harberger Tax on wallets.
- $UBI
- @TornadoCash-like ZKP Privacy

This is how it works:

Each "human" can privately register one or more "wallets" that handle funds. 

They also declare the value controlled by the wallet and pay a 1% tax on the declared value per year.

Wallets are always under auction with the declared value as the minimum bid.

The tax revenue funds the $UBI token by buying and burning it on DeFI.

$UBI tokens are distributed evenly among all humans as they currently are.

Then, and very importantly: It should be impossible to know which human owns which wallet(s).

To this end, funds from wallets are deposited and withdrawn @TornadoCash-like: in fixed sizes, e.g. 1, 10 and 100 ETH or 1k, 10k 100k and might support other tokens, like $DAI.

When asking for a payment, humans can "guide" the payment to reach their wallet, but the paying party won't know which wallet that will be.

The paying party will only know that the funds will reach a tax-paying wallet.
Finally, we wait to see if the social consensus becomes that tax-paying wallets are what is expected from a responsible human.

That's it.

I don't know enough about ZKP (yet) to know how to build this, but I wish to think it's possible.

A simple example of why I think that claiming that @Stacks inherits bitcoin security is misleading. 👇

Please correct me if I'm wrong. https://t.co/e87piohhR1

I understand that the stacks chain will observe the BTC tx, say from my address A to an address B, and identify any tx to address B as a new loan position. 

But then the question is: What if something happens to the stacks chain in the meanwhile?

- stacks network could suffer an attack, or
- miners could go bankrupt and stop mining, or
- miners could collude to decide to steal all the BTC controlled under the stacks chain (in accounts like address B)
In the end, the only way the BTC sent to address B will go out of address B is if the private key of address B signs the tx on the BTC network. Isn't it?

So, after the stacks chain and its miners go down, BTC remains up and healthy, but my BTC is lost/locked in address B.

Is this scenario impossible @muneeb? if so, how?

If it's not impossible, then, why is it claimed that stacks inherit BTC security?

For me, security means I won't lose my funds unless the BTC network is compromised.

My experience investing through "regulated" entities. @eToro in this case. Summary: it's stupid. 

So, I decided to buy 1kUSD-worth of Twitter shares on Jan 2021 on Etoro. 

That step seemed to "work". Or so I thought! It ended up it was a lie. I bought nothing actually.

(1/n)

At some point in time, the TWTR price fell sharply. I'm was ok with it. I bought it because I believed in the long-term value of the platform. A real Hodler. 

But then @etoro decided to sell some of "my" shares to protect "me" from losses. I lost a bunch of them here.

(2/n)

They did this without asking and they didn't even sent a notification. I saw it by login to their platform a few months later.

BTW, the platform is extremely limited. You can't see your order history for instance. How is that even possible?

(3/n)

Now @elonmusk buys the company, and eToro had sold the rest of "my" shares as part of the process. Didn't ask this time neither, of course. 

I now have 600 USD on their platform.

So what did I learn?

(4/n)

The shares you buy using investing platforms are not yours. 

I would have been much much better had I bought a token using @Uniswap (that dangerous unregulated platform).

My "shares" would have indeed been mine, and I would happily be a shareholder of TWTR today.

(end)

Some personal updates! After taking a 4-month break from my work as a dev, I'm ready to dive back into building.

I'm looking to join a fast-paced team of creators who are working on something ambitious (and useful :D), preferably in web3.

Thanks for the likes and retweets 🚀

Over the last 7 years, I've created and launched three products, all from idea to production.

I founded two of them (@uprtcl and @collectiveonepr), and the third, @commonvalue_xyz, was developed within @DAOstack.

For all the details, visit https://t.co/4HRnWJ9UZe.

After releasing @commonvalue_xyz, unfortunately, DAOstack had to halt all of its emerging projects, including us.

I decided to step away and focus on a personal project in the brick-and-mortar domain. The renovation of our house. I truly loved this experience 👷 https://t.co/Ze2IsEtFLZ
After that, I took some time off to be with my family. We had a wonderful time in Colombia. Here are some pics 🧡 https://t.co/X6aEjj6dvR

Now, I'm back. I've realized that while ideas and prototypes might seem challenging, they're actually the easy part. So, I want to focus on what's truly challenging:

Building something people genuinely want to use.

I've been involved with Web3 tech for 5 years and am fully convinced of its potential. I'd love to contribute to proving its worth.

If your team is hiring, do reach out!

In the meantime, apart from researching and networking, I'll be crafting and launching prototype ideas—aiming for one every two weeks.

This approach will help me get back into the groove and acquaint myself with new technologies.

I'll share the journey here.

Today, I'm starting my first prototype: A simple-as-possible payments app on Android that integrates @Circle USDC. 

The goal: understand how feasible (or unfeasible) this is.

The strategy. Keep it simple. Do not mention blockchain anywhere. Don't ask for seed phrases.

This will also serve as the template to build more fun features on top of it.

I'm starting by following this tutorial on @Circle docs:
https://t.co/yrTgoGPY6n

I have not built an Android App before. Let's see how it goes.
@jbrukh https://t.co/NYEaC8OA2P

Today I got the sample Android app from @Circle running and understood what it offers:

Circle will manage one or more External Accounts (EOAs) for each of *your* users and will transact, on-chain, for them.

The private keys of these EOAs are derived from a 6-digit numeric pin, chosen by the user, or can be recovered by answering two security questions.

So it does abstract the private key/seed storage issue to some degree.
I imagine only Circle can derive the private keys from a PIN using some internal secret they keep, so these EAOs are controlled by the users, but "gated" by Circle.

Unfortunately, that's all. 

I thought this would bring me closer to adding a FIAT on-ramp inside the app, but it does not.
FIAT on-ramp is still very hard. It seems it must be solved on a per-country basis using USDC partners (a.k.a. exchanges) so I won't be able to add it to my app.
My next step will be to program the app to show the balance of USDC held by a user, and to transfer it.

Here I'm a contrarian. I think that, as everything else that is hard in life, learning to manage a mobile device in conjunction with the real world is an ability that is learnt best the *early you start*. Like playing piano.

I gave my kid a tablet at 4 yo and taught him the challenges it comes with. How it influences your brain and makes you not want to stop using it. He also learned how many different things can be done with a mobile phone. Calling and texting cousins and grandparents, learning, translating, playing, entertaining, doom scrolling....etc

In addition, forbidding or setting a usage schedule will actually teach him that you should use the mobile phone for every minute you are allowed to. Instead, not forbidding teaches him that is up to him to control the device, not someone else responsibility.

He is 7 now. He is the one that helps *me* control my mobile phone addiction. He? He uses his tablet very sporadically. 

Right now he is reading a book while I write this tweet from my mobile...

"DAO"what?

The term “DAO” is doing more harm than good to the growth of blockchain-powered communities.

A thread🧵

Most active DAOs today lie within one of the following types: 

- treasury-driven DAOs.
- service-provider DAOs. 

And none of them really exploit the key value proposition brought by blockchain tech to most communities outside the crypto-niche.

Treasury-driven DAOs, which represent most of the active ones, are actually an anomaly.

On one side, let’s be honest, they are held together by the treasury, nothing else, and, on the other, they emerge out of an uncommon situation: an excess of money 🙃
Using treasury-driven DAOs as a reference on how to grow and manage blockchain-powered communities beyond the crypto-niche is just counter-productive.

The other type is “service DAOs”, like @dOrg_tech  and others, and these do represent a better use case. 

Using a blockchain to transparently handle money within a group of semi-independent teams is useful.

Adding decision-making and reputation might be cool too.

The problem is that service DAOs ask for deep and long-term commitments to be really useful, and, in the end, if they end up being run as a normal consultancy firm, the value of the blockchain becomes less clear.
So, is blockchain tech useless for normal communities? How can this be possible? We thought blockchain would enable a completely new set of coordination patterns, didn't we?

Maybe that is the problem! 

We were so excited about the possibilities of DAOs that we went straight for the utopian vision, thinking that we could skip any intermediary steps from the status quo to a fully decentralized future without corporations or decadent institutions.

In retrospect, it was obvious we were going to fail :)

What can we do then?

Shift the perspective. 

Instead of designing abstract solutions that enable new types of organizations, we should go and meet groups and communities where they are and offer them solutions that are both very useful for them and only possible because of blockchain tech.
This might look a bit boring. We, blockchain visionaries, lowering down to the boring world of normal people 😁.

But you can bet on it. It's 100x harder to find a real problem and build a solution to it than it is to envision a complex governance system designed to work on hypothetical decentralized communities that exist only in our imagination.
This is why my current focus is on finding what is it that blockchain tech can offer to normal groups of people that no other tech can offer them.

The answer is quite surprising: A lot! 

Blockchain can offer unique solutions to groups and communities that are just impossible without the tech.

I can think of two things here:

1) Handle money openly and transparently
2) Use cryptography for privacy

Handling money by a group of people requires a legal entity + a bank account + a lot of bureaucracy. 

And even then, the process is opaque and hidden to most members of the community.

Collectively handling a stablecoin with trustless processes would be so convenient.
It's so obvious, but, yet, you cannot find teams building DAO-tooling who are laser-focused on this.

Then there is ZK-tech, which allows members of the community to communicate anonymously among themselves without relying on web2 platforms and their identities.

This can be a critical feature in environments where people are afraid to talk and act.

Voting, or aggregating the community sentiment around a given issue, anonymously, would make this so much more useful, and is now totally possible using dev-friendly tools like Semaphore from @PrivacyScaling.
These two features by themselves potentially offer a lot of value to communities, but they are only useful as long as the technology is accessible to normal people.

This means that, just as for the rest of web3 products, tools for communities need to fix the onboarding problem. 

Here is where account abstraction and cloud wallet services become essential.

Members of a group should not need to set up a wallet or, god forbid, buy crypto, to start participating. They should just need a mobile phone, that’s it.

The good news is that all the pieces needed are already there with services like @magic_labs or @privy_io.

The resulting landscape becomes pretty exciting, then! 

There are millions of groups or would-be groups around the world that could really use this technology, if only we, as builders, make it easy for them to do so.
Let’s do it then! Let’s just keep it simple for now.
